# Vendor Contract — Mandatory Clauses Under Indian Law

## Clause: GST Compliance and Tax Invoice Requirements
Act: Central Goods and Services Tax Act 2017, Section 31
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: Every registered supplier must issue a tax invoice with: supplier GSTIN, recipient GSTIN (if registered), invoice number, date, HSN/SAC code, taxable value, GST rate and amount. Failure to issue proper tax invoices disallows input tax credit to the buyer.
Standard counter-clause: "The Vendor shall issue GST-compliant tax invoices in accordance with Section 31 of the CGST Act 2017. Each invoice shall include the Vendor's GSTIN, HSN/SAC codes, taxable value, applicable GST rate, and the Vendor's authorized signature. The Vendor shall file GST returns timely to enable the Client's input tax credit."

## Clause: TDS Deduction and PAN Verification
Act: Income Tax Act 1961, Sections 194C, 194J
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: Payments to contractors (194C: 1%/2% TDS) and professionals (194J: 10% TDS) require TDS deduction at source. The vendor must provide PAN; payments to non-PAN holders attract 20% TDS. Form 16A must be issued quarterly.
Standard counter-clause: "The Client shall deduct Tax at Source (TDS) from payments to the Vendor as per the applicable rate under the Income Tax Act 1961. The Vendor shall provide their valid PAN. The Client shall issue Form 16A within fifteen days of the due date of filing the TDS return for each quarter."

## Clause: MSME Payment Timeline Compliance
Act: Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises Development Act 2006, Section 15
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: If the supplier is an MSME (registered under MSMED Act), payment must be made within 45 days of acceptance of goods/services (or 15 days if no written agreement). Delayed payment attracts compound interest at 3× the RBI bank rate (currently ~13.5% per annum). The buyer must also disclose outstanding MSME payments in their balance sheet.
Standard counter-clause: "Where the Vendor is a Micro or Small Enterprise registered under the MSMED Act 2006, the Client shall make payment within forty-five days of acceptance of goods or services as defined under Section 15 of the MSMED Act. Delayed payment shall attract compound interest as prescribed under Section 16 of the Act."

## Clause: Purchase Order and Acceptance Mechanism
Act: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 7 (acceptance); CGST Act 2017
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: A vendor agreement must define the process for placing orders, acceptance criteria for goods/services, and the timeline for raising disputes about quality. Without this, acceptance disputes arise affecting payment timelines and GST obligations.
Standard counter-clause: "The Client shall issue Purchase Orders for each engagement. The Vendor shall commence work only after written acknowledgment of the PO. Goods or services shall be deemed accepted unless the Client raises written objections within fifteen working days of delivery."

## Clause: Warranty and Defect Liability Period
Act: Sale of Goods Act 1930, Sections 12–16; Consumer Protection Act 2019 (for B2C)
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: Implied warranties of merchantability and fitness for purpose apply to all goods under the Sale of Goods Act. For services, the standard is reasonable care and skill. A vendor agreement should make defect liability periods explicit rather than relying on implied warranties.
Standard counter-clause: "The Vendor warrants that all goods supplied shall be of merchantable quality, free from material defects, and fit for the intended purpose for a period of twelve months from delivery. All services shall be performed with reasonable care and skill. Defects notified within the warranty period shall be rectified at no additional cost."

## Clause: Indemnification for IP Infringement
Act: Copyright Act 1957; Trade Marks Act 1999; Patents Act 1970
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: If the vendor uses third-party licensed software, content, or patented processes in their deliverable, the client is exposed to infringement liability. The vendor must indemnify for any IP infringement in the delivered work.
Standard counter-clause: "The Vendor shall indemnify and hold harmless the Client against all claims, damages, costs, and legal fees arising from any allegation that the deliverables infringe any third-party intellectual property rights. The Vendor represents that all deliverables are original or that the Vendor holds valid licenses for all third-party components."

## Clause: Confidentiality of Client Data and NDA
Act: Information Technology Act 2000; IT (Amendment) Act 2008; Indian Contract Act 1872
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: A vendor with access to client's business data, customer information, or trade secrets must be bound by explicit confidentiality obligations. The IT Act imposes penalties for unauthorized disclosure of personal data. A mutual NDA should be embedded in the vendor agreement.
Standard counter-clause: "The Vendor shall maintain strict confidentiality of all Client data, customer information, trade secrets, pricing, and business processes accessed during this engagement. This obligation survives termination for a period of three years. The Vendor shall not store or process Client data outside India without prior written consent."

## Clause: Limitation of Liability Cap
Act: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 73–74; relevant case law
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: A vendor agreement should explicitly set a liability cap proportionate to the contract value to prevent disproportionate damage claims. Without a cap, a vendor could face unlimited consequential loss claims. Indian courts have enforced reasonable contractual liability caps.
Standard counter-clause: "The total aggregate liability of either party for any claims under this agreement, whether in contract, tort, or otherwise, shall not exceed the total fees paid by the Client to the Vendor in the twelve months immediately preceding the event giving rise to the claim. Neither party shall be liable for indirect, consequential, special, or punitive damages."

## Clause: Termination for Convenience Notice Period
Act: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 73
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: A termination for convenience clause should specify adequate notice and compensation for work-in-progress costs already incurred by the vendor. A clause allowing immediate termination at any time without compensation may be challenged as a penalty clause.
Standard counter-clause: "The Client may terminate this agreement for convenience by giving thirty days' written notice. Upon such termination, the Client shall pay the Vendor for all work completed and reasonable costs incurred up to the date of termination, including non-cancellable commitments made in reliance on this agreement."

## Clause: Dispute Resolution Mechanism
Act: Arbitration and Conciliation Act 1996; Commercial Courts Act 2015
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: Vendor agreements should specify a dispute resolution mechanism. For commercial disputes of Rs. 3 lakh or more, mandatory pre-litigation mediation under the Commercial Courts Act 2015 applies. Arbitration clauses should specify seat, rules, and number of arbitrators.
Standard counter-clause: "Any dispute shall first be attempted to be resolved through negotiation within 30 days of written notice. If unresolved, it shall be referred to mediation. If mediation fails, the dispute shall be resolved through arbitration in [city] under the Arbitration and Conciliation Act 1996, before a sole arbitrator mutually appointed. The language of arbitration shall be English."

## Clause: Insurance Requirements
Act: Workmen's Compensation Act 1923; Motor Vehicles Act 1988; applicable professional indemnity norms
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: For service vendors engaging employees or using vehicles at the client's site, workmen's compensation insurance is mandatory. For professional services, professional indemnity insurance is recommended best practice. The contract should specify minimum coverage amounts.
Standard counter-clause: "The Vendor shall maintain, at its own cost, throughout the term: (a) Workmen's Compensation insurance as required by law for all its employees; (b) General commercial liability insurance of not less than INR [amount]; (c) Professional Indemnity insurance of not less than INR [amount] where applicable. Proof of insurance shall be provided annually."

## Clause: Background Verification of Vendor Personnel
Act: IT Act 2000; SEBI and RBI guidelines for financial sector; various state-specific regulations
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: For vendors deploying personnel at client sites or handling sensitive data, the client should contractually require background verification (criminal record, academic credentials, employment history checks). This protects against insider threats and regulatory compliance.
Standard counter-clause: "The Vendor shall conduct background verification (including criminal record check, academic credential verification, and previous employment verification) for all personnel deployed at Client's premises or granted access to Client's systems, prior to such deployment. Records shall be provided to the Client on request."

## Clause: Data Localization and Cross-Border Transfer
Act: Information Technology Act 2000, Section 43A; IT (Reasonable Security Practices) Rules 2011; upcoming Personal Data Protection Bill
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: Sensitive personal data (financial, health, biometric) must be stored in India under current IT Rules. Cross-border transfer requires explicit consent and equivalent protection in the destination country. The vendor agreement should specify data residence requirements.
Standard counter-clause: "Client data including any sensitive personal data as defined under the IT (Reasonable Security Practices and Procedures and Sensitive Personal Data or Information) Rules 2011 shall be stored, processed, and retained within the territory of India. Any cross-border transfer requires prior written consent of the Client and compliance with applicable data protection laws."

## Clause: Vendor Audit Rights
Act: Companies Act 2013, Section 143 (for statutory audits); contractual right as per Indian Contract Act 1872
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: For significant vendor relationships, the client should retain audit rights to verify compliance with contractual terms, data security standards, and financial records (especially for cost-plus contracts). This is particularly important for regulated industries (banking, insurance, pharma).
Standard counter-clause: "The Client shall have the right, upon fifteen days' written notice, to audit the Vendor's records, systems, and facilities to the extent reasonably necessary to verify compliance with this agreement. The Vendor shall cooperate with such audits and provide all reasonably requested information. The cost of such audit shall be borne by the Client unless material non-compliance is found."

## Clause: Force Majeure — Exclusion from Payment Obligations
Act: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 56 (frustration of contract); Force Majeure jurisprudence
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: Force majeure clauses typically excuse performance delays but Indian courts have generally held that payment obligations (already accrued) are not discharged by force majeure. The contract should clarify that payment for work already performed remains due regardless of force majeure.
Standard counter-clause: "If either party is prevented from performing its obligations due to force majeure events (acts of God, war, government orders, natural disasters, epidemics), that party shall notify the other within 7 days and performance shall be excused for the duration. However, the Client's obligation to pay for services already rendered before the force majeure event shall not be suspended or waived."

## Clause: Applicable Governing Law
Act: Indian Contract Act 1872; Civil Procedure Code 1908
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: The agreement must specify the governing law (typically laws of India) and the jurisdiction for dispute resolution. For cross-border vendor agreements, the choice of law clause should specifically address which country's law applies and must comply with Indian conflict of laws rules.
Standard counter-clause: "This agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of India. The courts of [city] shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any disputes not resolved through the dispute resolution mechanism in Clause [X], subject to the arbitration clause where applicable."
